message.content:
Repo: /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-1, fixes applied vs main). Venv: /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
The file /tmp/pass1_confirm.json is a JSON array of 22 pass-1 findings (each: issue, title, file, sev, problem, suggested_fix).
Constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign, no metered API, engine imports no adapter, lazy optional backends.

You are independent CONFIRMER #2 of 2 for finding INDEX 18 (0-based) in /tmp/pass1_confirm.json.
Read that item (its issue number, problem, suggested_fix, file). Then VERIFY THE APPLIED FIX on the current branch: read the changed source AND its regression test (git diff vs main, or just read the file + tests), and where useful run a PoC against the venv to confirm the original problem is actually resolved.
Return: appropriate = the fix correctly addresses the root cause in a sound way (not a hack/incomplete patch/over-claim); complete = the fix fully resolves the finding AND has a regression test that would catch a regression. Set either false if not, and say exactly what is missing. Echo the issue number in 'issue'.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the finding at index 18.

content.content:
{
  "issue": "119",
  "title": "corpus_fingerprint is recomputed redundantly per study (ingest + every build_baseline_artifact call), each a full canonical copy + sort + JSON of the whole corpus",
  "file": "src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py",
  "sev": "LOW",
  "problem": "corpus_fingerprint builds a full list-comprehension copy of every unit and relation, sorts all units by id, canonicalizes every attribute mapping via _plain, and json.dumps the whole thing \u2014 O(total corpus bytes) time and memory. run_study computes it once at ingest (pipeline.py:280, `cfp`). Then build_baseline_artifact (determinism.py:104) computes it again from scratch for the determinism record's `input_hashes['corpus']` and `corpus_fingerprint`; a study's BaselineBuilder that calls build_baseline_artifact thus repeats the full-corpus canonicalization a second (and the builder may itself fi",
  "suggested_fix": "Compute corpus_fingerprint once and pass it into build_baseline_artifact (e.g. an optional `corpus_hash` arg) instead of recomputing; or memoize on the Corpus instance (it is frozen/immutable). Threading the already-computed `cfp` from run_study into the determinism harness removes the duplicate full-corpus pass entirely."
}

diff --git a/src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py b/src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py
index de8af5a..4b81b64 100644
--- a/src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py
+++ b/src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py
@@ -92,6 +92,7 @@ def build_baseline_artifact(
     component_versions: Mapping[str, str] | None = None,
     extra_inputs: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None,
     seed: int | None = None,
+    corpus_hash: str | None = None,
 ) -> BaselineArtifact:
     """Assemble a :class:`BaselineArtifact` with a complete determinism record.
 
@@ -99,9 +100,12 @@ def build_baseline_artifact(
     ``component_versions`` records the builder + any model/library versions (the engine and
     Python versions are added automatically). ``extra_inputs`` are additional inputs to hash
     (e.g. config). ``seed``, if omitted, is derived deterministically from the corpus + inputs
-    so it is reproducible.
+    so it is reproducible. ``corpus_hash`` lets a caller pass an already-computed
+    :func:`corpus_fingerprint` to avoid recomputing it (#119).
     """
-    corpus_hash = corpus_fingerprint(corpus)
+    # Reuse a precomputed fingerprint if the caller already has one (e.g. the runner computed it
+    # at ingest), avoiding a redundant full canonical-hash of the whole corpus (#119).
+    corpus_hash = corpus_hash if corpus_hash is not None else corpus_fingerprint(corpus)
     if extra_inputs and "corpus" in extra_inputs:
         raise ValueError("'corpus' is a reserved input-hash key — rename the extra input (#B7)")
     input_hashes = {"corpus": corpus_hash}

content.text: The fix adds an optional `corpus_hash` param. Now let me verify the caller in pipeline.py actually threads `cfp` through, and check for a regression test.

The duplicate computation the finding describes is inside the builder. Let me check how the builder calls `build_baseline_artifact` and whether the threading actually happens there.

content.text: `tests/test_baseline.py` has 27 added lines. Let me look at them to see if they cover the `corpus_hash` parameter.

content.content:
diff --git a/tests/test_baseline.py b/tests/test_baseline.py
index 78864b9..361ae41 100644
--- a/tests/test_baseline.py
+++ b/tests/test_baseline.py
@@ -251,3 +251,30 @@ def test_cosine_matrix_rejects_norm_overflow():
 
     with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="overflow"):
         _cosine_matrix_numpy([[1e200, 1e200], [1e200, -1e200]])
+
+
+def test_cosine_matrix_array_matches_list_and_is_ndarray():
+    # #106: the ndarray variant avoids nested-list boxing but agrees with the list form
+    np = pytest.importorskip("numpy")
+    from assay_engine.baseline.primitives import (
+        cosine_similarity_matrix,
+        cosine_similarity_matrix_array,
+    )
+
+    rows = [[1.0, 0.0], [0.0, 1.0], [1.0, 1.0]]
+    arr = cosine_similarity_matrix_array(rows)
+    assert isinstance(arr, np.ndarray) and arr.shape == (3, 3)
+    assert arr.tolist() == cosine_similarity_matrix(rows)
+    assert cosine_similarity_matrix_array([]).shape == (0, 0)
+
+
+def test_freeze_keeps_ndarray_opaque_o1():
+    # #107: a numpy array in baseline contents is frozen to a small (kind, shape, bytes)
+    # descriptor — NOT a recursively tuple-ized cell-by-cell copy.
+    np = pytest.importorskip("numpy")
+    from assay_engine._frozen import freeze
+
+    frozen = freeze(np.zeros((100, 100)))
+    # descriptor form: a 3-tuple (kind, shape, frozen-bytes), not a 100-deep nested structure
+    assert isinstance(frozen, tuple) and len(frozen) == 3
+    assert frozen[1] == (100, 100)

content.text:
The added tests in `test_baseline.py` cover findings #106 and #107, NOT #119. There is no regression test for the `corpus_hash` parameter threading.

Let me verify the fix itself is sound and confirm no test exercises the new param anywhere, and write a quick PoC.

content.text: `corpus_hash` appears ONLY in `determinism.py`. No production caller (pipeline, builder, reference_study) actually passes it, and no test exercises it. Let me confirm the seed-derivation path uses the precomputed hash correctly (so passing it produces identical output), and run a PoC.

content.content:

107	    # at ingest), avoiding a redundant full canonical-hash of the whole corpus (#119).
108	    corpus_hash = corpus_hash if corpus_hash is not None else corpus_fingerprint(corpus)

116	    versions.update(component_versions or {})
117

119	        # Derive from all input hashes (the corpus hash is already among them under "corpus").
120	        seed = stable_seed(*(input_hashes[k] for k in sorted(input_hashes)))
121	
122	    record = DeterminismRecord(seed=seed, input_hashes=input_hashes, component_versions=versions)
123	    return BaselineArtifact(
124	        corpus_fingerprint=corpus_hash, contents=contents, determinism=record.as_dict()
125	    )

content.text: The fix is functionally sound: when `corpus_hash` is passed, it's used both as `input_hashes["corpus"]` (feeding seed derivation) and the artifact's `corpus_fingerprint`, producing output identical to recomputing — provided the caller passes the actual `corpus_fingerprint(corpus)`. Let me run a PoC to confirm passing the precomputed hash yields an identical artifact to letting it recompute.

input.command:
cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && cat > /tmp/poc119.py <<'EOF'
from tests.test_baseline import _corpus
from assay_engine.baseline.determinism import build_baseline_artifact, corpus_fingerprint

c = _corpus()
cfp = corpus_fingerprint(c)
a1 = build_baseline_artifact(c, {"sim": [[1.0]]}, component_versions={"builder": "demo@1"})
a2 = build_baseline_artifact(c, {"sim": [[1.0]]}, component_versions={"builder": "demo@1"}, corpus_hash=cfp)
print("recompute fp:", a1.corpus_fingerprint)
print("passed   fp:", a2.corpus_fingerprint)
print("determinism equal:", a1.determinism == a2.determinism)
print("fp equal:", a1.corpus_fingerprint == a2.corpus_fingerprint)
# wrong hash should propagate (proves it's actually used, no recompute override)
a3 = build_baseline_artifact(c, {"sim": [[1.0]]}, component_versions={"builder": "demo@1"}, corpus_hash="BOGUS")
print("bogus used (no silent recompute):", a3.corpus_fingerprint == "BOGUS")
EOF

content.text:
PoC confirms the fix works correctly: passing a precomputed `corpus_hash` yields an identical artifact (same fingerprint, same determinism/seed), and the passed value is genuinely used (no silent recompute).

Now the key gaps:
1. **Completeness of the perf goal**: The finding's core complaint is the *duplicate full-corpus pass* in `run_study`'s flow. But the fix only adds an *optional parameter* to `build_baseline_artifact`. No production caller passes it — `pipeline.py` computes `cfp` but never threads it into `build_baseline_artifact` (the pipeline consumes a `baseline` built by an external `BaselineBuilder`, and that builder doesn't accept/forward `cfp` either). So the redundant computation the finding describes is **not actually eliminated** in any real code path; the fix only enables it.
2. **No regression test** for #119. The 27 added lines in test_baseline.py cover #106/#107. Nothing tests the `corpus_hash` parameter.

Let me confirm the severity context — this is LOW, and verify the builder truly has no path to thread it.

content.content:

41	
42	
43	class BaselineBuilder(Protocol):
44	    """Build a :class:`BaselineArtifact` from a corpus, blind to any external claims."""
45	
46	    def build(
47	        self, corpus: Corpus, *, claim_guard: ClaimBlindGuard[ExternalClaimsSource]
48	    ) -> BaselineArtifact:
49	        """Construct the baseline. Must run with ``claim_guard.sealed()`` in force."""
50	        ...
51

content.text:
`BaselineBuilder` is a Protocol whose `build` signature takes only `corpus` and `claim_guard` — it has no way to receive a precomputed `cfp`. So even the architecture doesn't let `run_study` thread its `cfp` to the user-supplied builder. The fix is purely an enabling hook on the lower-level helper; the actual redundant pass described in the finding remains in any builder that uses `build_baseline_artifact`.

I have enough to render a verdict.

input.reasoning:
The applied fix (src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py:95,108,124) adds an optional `corpus_hash: str | None = None` parameter to build_baseline_artifact, using a precomputed fingerprint when supplied and falling back to corpus_fingerprint(corpus) otherwise. The approach is sound and matches the suggested_fix's "optional corpus_hash arg" path: the passed value flows into both input_hashes["corpus"] (and therefore the seed derivation) and the artifact's corpus_fingerprint, so passing the true fingerprint is behaviorally identical to recomputing. My PoC against the venv confirms this — artifact built with the precomputed cfp is byte-identical (same fingerprint, same determinism record/seed) to one that recomputes, and a deliberately wrong corpus_hash propagates through (proving the param is actually consumed, no silent recompute). So appropriate = true: it correctly targets the root cause without breaking determinism.

complete = false for two reasons. (1) The fix only ENABLES avoiding the duplicate pass; it does not actually eliminate the redundant full-corpus canonicalization that the finding centers on. No production code path passes corpus_hash: pipeline.py computes `cfp` at line 299 but never threads it into build_baseline_artifact, because run_study consumes a BaselineArtifact produced by an external BaselineBuilder, and the BaselineBuilder Protocol's build(corpus, *, claim_guard) signature (toolkit.py:46-49) has no parameter to receive the precomputed cfp and no forwarding to build_baseline_artifact. grep shows `corpus_hash` exists only inside determinism.py. So the duplicate O(corpus-bytes) pass the finding describes still occurs in every real builder; the suggested_fix's stated goal ("Threading the already-computed cfp from run_study into the determinism harness removes the duplicate full-corpus pass entirely") is NOT achieved. (2) There is no regression test for the new parameter — the 27 added lines in tests/test_baseline.py cover findings #106 and #107 (cosine_similarity_matrix_array / ndarray freeze), and grep finds no test referencing corpus_hash or #119 anywhere. A future change could drop or break the corpus_hash short-circuit with no test failing.
input.evidence: src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py:95,108,124 (new corpus_hash param + reuse). pipeline.py:299 computes cfp; grep over src/+tests shows `corpus_hash` appears ONLY in determinism.py (no caller threads it). BaselineBuilder Protocol toolkit.py:46-49 has no cfp param. git diff main tests/test_baseline.py adds only tests for #106/#107 (cosine_similarity_matrix_array, freeze ndarray); no #119/corpus_hash test. PoC /tmp/poc119.py: recompute fp == passed fp == c217d569...; determinism equal: True; bogus hash propagates (used, no recompute).

content.text:
Verdict recorded for finding #119.

**appropriate = true, complete = false.**

The fix adds an optional `corpus_hash` param to `build_baseline_artifact` (determinism.py:95/108/124) — a sound, behavior-preserving hook (PoC confirmed identical artifacts and that the passed value is genuinely used). But it only *enables* avoiding the duplicate pass: no production caller threads it (grep shows `corpus_hash` lives only in determinism.py; pipeline.py computes `cfp` but never passes it, and the `BaselineBuilder.build` Protocol has no param to forward it), so the redundant full-corpus canonicalization the finding describes still occurs in real code paths. Additionally there is no regression test for #119 — the new test_baseline.py lines cover #106/#107 only.
